title: Linear Equations
blurb: Multi-step equations, variables on both sides, and equations with no solution or infinitely many.

lessons.title: Two-Step Equations
lessons.concreteComparison: A taxi charges a $\$3$ flat fee plus $\$2$ per mile, and your ride cost $\$11$. How many miles? The equation is $2x+3=11$. To find $x$, peel the costs off in **reverse order**: first remove the flat fee ($11-3=8$), then undo the per-mile rate ($8\div2=4$). Four miles — solved by working backwards.
lessons.objective: Solve two-step equations like $2x+3=11$ by undoing addition/subtraction first, then multiplication/division — and check by substituting back.
concept: A **two-step equation** wraps $x$ in two operations: $2x+3=11$ multiplies by $2$, then adds $3$. Solving **unwraps in reverse**: subtract $3$ first, then divide by $2$.
Every move must hit **both sides** — an equation is a balanced scale, and whatever you do to one pan you must do to the other.
**Always check**: substitute back. $2(4)+3=11$ ✓. A ten-second check catches nearly every slip.
lessons.keyIdea: Undo in reverse order — add/subtract first, then multiply/divide — doing the same to both sides.

facts.title: Both sides
facts.explanation: Every operation applies to both pans.

commonMistakes.correction: In $2x+3=11$, dividing everything by 2 first forces fractions ($x+1.5=5.5$) — legal but messy. Strip the +3 first.

commonMistakes.text: Doing an operation to only one side.
commonMistakes.correction: $2x+3=11 \to 2x=11-3$ is fine, but forgetting to change the right side breaks the balance.

steps.title: The scale never lies
steps.teach: The scale shows $x+3=7$. Take $3$ off **both** pans and the box alone balances $4$ — that's the whole idea of solving.

steps.title: Unwrap in reverse
beats: To free it, undo in reverse: **−3**, then **÷2**.
Like unwrapping a gift: last layer on, first layer off.

steps.title: First move?
steps.explanation: Strip the +5 first: $3x=15$, then divide to get $x=5$.

steps.title: Two steps, both sides
steps.intro: Undo the add/subtract, then the multiply/divide.

steps.title: al-jabr
steps.body: The word **algebra** comes from “al-jabr” — “the reunion of broken parts” — in the title of a 9th-century book by al-Khwarizmi of Baghdad. His method was exactly what you just did: restore balance by doing the same thing to both sides. His own name, Latinized, became the word **algorithm**.

lessons.title: Simplify Before Solving
lessons.concreteComparison: Two friends split a job: one folds $2$ boxes a minute, the other $7$. Together they folded $18$ boxes: $2x+7x=18$. Before touching both sides, tidy the left: $2x+7x$ is just $9x$. Then $9x=18$ falls in one step: $x=2$ minutes. **Combine like terms first**, and a messy equation becomes a short one.
lessons.objective: Combine like terms on each side before solving, turning cluttered equations like $2x+7x=18$ or $5x-2x+4=13$ into one- or two-step equations.
concept: **Like terms** share the same variable part: $2x$ and $7x$ are like terms; $2x$ and $7$ are not. Combine them by adding coefficients: $2x+7x=9x$.
**Simplify each side first**, then solve. $2x+7x=18 \to 9x=18 \to x=2$.
Watch subtraction: $5x-2x+4=13 \to 3x+4=13 \to 3x=9 \to x=3$. The sign belongs to the term after it.
Constants combine with constants: $4x+3+5=20 \to 4x+8=20$.
lessons.keyIdea: Tidy each side first — combine like terms — then unwrap as usual.

facts.title: Like terms
facts.explanation: Same variable part → add the coefficients.

facts.title: Then solve
facts.explanation: The tidied equation is one step.

commonMistakes.correction: $2x$ and $7$ aren't like terms — one has an $x$, one doesn't. Only $2x+7x$ makes $9x$.
$5x-2x=3x$, not $7x$ — the minus travels with the $2x$.

steps.title: Like terms combine
beats: But $2x+7$ stays as is — $7$ has no $x$.
Rule: add coefficients of the **same** variable part.

steps.title: Simplify, then unwrap
steps.intro: Combine like terms on each side first.

steps.title: Why mathematicians are lazy (on purpose)
steps.body: Combining $2x+7x$ into $9x$ before solving isn't just neatness — it's a core habit called **reducing the problem**. Great problem-solvers spend their first minutes making the problem smaller, not attacking it. One tidy line often saves ten messy ones.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Like terms share a variable part: $2x+7x=9x$.

lessons.title: Variables on Both Sides
lessons.concreteComparison: Two phone plans: Plan A costs $\$5$ up front plus $\$3$ per GB; Plan B costs $\$15$ plus $\$1$ per GB. When do they cost the same? $3x+5=x+15$. There's an $x$ on **each side** — so first herd all the $x$s to one side, all the numbers to the other, and the crossover point appears: $x=5$ GB.
lessons.objective: Solve equations with variables on both sides by collecting variable terms on one side and constants on the other.
concept: When $x$ appears on both sides, **collect** first: subtract the smaller variable term from both sides. $3x+5=x+15 \to 2x+5=15$.
Then it's a familiar two-step: $2x=10$, so $x=5$.
Choosing to remove the **smaller** $x$-term keeps the coefficient positive — fewer sign slips.
Check in the ORIGINAL equation: $3(5)+5=20$ and $5+15=20$ ✓ — both sides agree.
lessons.keyIdea: Move all $x$s to one side and all constants to the other, then unwrap.

facts.title: Collect variables
facts.explanation: Subtract the smaller x-term from both sides.

commonMistakes.text: Combining terms ACROSS the equals sign.
commonMistakes.correction: In $3x+5=x+15$ you can't add $3x$ and $x$ directly — they're on different sides. Move one first.

commonMistakes.text: Moving a term without flipping its sign.
commonMistakes.correction: Bringing $x$ across turns it into $-x$: $3x+5=x+15$ becomes $3x-x+5=15$.

steps.title: An x on each side
beats: Goal: all $x$s on one side, numbers on the other.
Now it's a two-step you already know.

steps.title: First collection
steps.prompt: Solving $5x+2=3x+10$, a good first move is…
steps.explanation: Removing the smaller x-term gives $2x+2=10$ — positive coefficient, smooth sailing.

steps.title: Herd the x's
steps.intro: Subtract the smaller variable term first.

steps.title: The break-even instinct
steps.body: “When do two deals cost the same?” is one of the most-used equations in real life — phone plans, gym memberships, rent vs buy, print vs digital. Businesses call the crossover the **break-even point**, and it's always a variables-on-both-sides equation.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Collect $x$s on one side, constants on the other.
Remove the **smaller** x-term to stay positive.
Check both sides of the ORIGINAL equation.

lessons.title: Equations with Parentheses
lessons.concreteComparison: Three gift bags, each holding a mystery toy plus $2$ candies, weigh $15$ oz total: $3(x+2)=15$. You can't touch the $x$ while it's locked in parentheses — so **distribute** first: $3x+6=15$. Now it's a plain two-step. Parentheses open, then the equation surrenders.
lessons.objective: Use the distributive property to clear parentheses, then solve — including equations that need distributing on both sides.
concept: **Distributive property:** $a(b+c)=ab+ac$. The outside number multiplies EVERY term inside: $3(x+2)=3x+6$.
Then solve normally: $3x+6=15 \to 3x=9 \to x=3$.
A negative distributes its sign: $-2(x-4)=-2x+8$ — both products flip.
Shortcut when the outside divides the other side evenly: $3(x+2)=15$ → divide both sides by 3 first: $x+2=5$, so $x=3$. Same answer, fewer steps.
lessons.keyIdea: Open the parentheses with the distributive property, then unwrap as usual.

commonMistakes.text: Distributing to only the first term.
commonMistakes.correction: $3(x+2)=3x+2$ is wrong — the 3 multiplies BOTH terms: $3x+6$.
The negative hits both terms: $-2\cdot x=-2x$ and $-2\cdot(-4)=+8$.

steps.title: Distribute to open
beats: Picture 3 bags, each with an $x$ and 2 candies:

steps.title: Open, then solve
steps.intro: Distribute first; both sides if needed.

steps.title: Why “distribute”?
steps.body: The name is literal: the outside number gets **distributed** — handed out — to every term inside, like dealing cards to every player. Skip a player and the game (or the algebra) breaks. It's the single most-used law in all of algebra.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Open parentheses first, then solve as usual.

lessons.title: One, None, or Infinitely Many
lessons.concreteComparison: Simplify an equation and sometimes the $x$ **vanishes**. If you're left with $5=5$ — always true — EVERY number works: infinitely many solutions. If you're left with $2=7$ — never true — NO number works. An equation isn't just a puzzle with one answer; it can be an identity, or an impossibility, and the algebra tells you which.
lessons.objective: Recognize when a linear equation has exactly one solution, no solution, or infinitely many — by simplifying and reading what remains.
concept: **One solution** (the usual case): the $x$s survive. $2x+3=11$ leaves $x=4$ — exactly one number works.
**No solution**: the $x$s cancel and what's left is FALSE. $x+5=x+9 \to 5=9$ ✗. No number can make it true.
**Infinitely many**: the $x$s cancel and what's left is TRUE. $2(x+3)=2x+6 \to 6=6$ ✓ for every $x$ — the two sides were the same expression in disguise (an **identity**).
Strategy: solve normally. If $x$ disappears, judge the leftover statement — true means all numbers, false means none.
lessons.keyIdea: If the x's cancel: leftover TRUE → infinitely many; leftover FALSE → no solution.

commonMistakes.text: Writing x=0 when the x's cancel.
commonMistakes.correction: $5=9$ has no $x$ left to equal zero — the verdict is about the leftover statement, not $x=0$.
$6=6$ means every $x$ works (infinitely many), the OPPOSITE of no solution.

steps.title: One solution
beats: Most equations you've met are like this.

steps.title: No solution
beats: No value of $x$ can rescue it.

steps.title: Judge it
steps.explanation: $1=5$ is false no matter what $x$ is — no solution. (Not $x=0$: check $1=5$ has no $x$ at all.)

steps.title: Count the solutions
steps.intro: Solve; if x vanishes, judge the leftover.

problems.explanation: Both sides are identical — every $x$ works.

steps.title: Parallel lines in disguise
steps.body: Next topic you'll graph both sides of an equation as two lines. One solution = the lines **cross once**. No solution = they're **parallel** (never meet). Infinitely many = they're the **same line**. The three endings you just learned are secretly a geometry fact.

lessons.title: From Words to Equations
lessons.concreteComparison: “A gym charges a $\$20$ sign-up fee plus $\$15$ a month. You've paid $\$95$ so far — how many months have you been a member?” No equation in sight — until you build one: $15m+20=95$. Translating words into algebra is the real superpower; the solving part you already own.
lessons.objective: Translate real situations into linear equations — identifying the unknown, the rate, and the constant — then solve and interpret the answer.
concept: **Name the unknown** first: let $m$ = months. A one-letter summary of what you're hunting.
**Find the structure**: a per-something rate multiplies the unknown ($15m$), a one-time amount stands alone ($+20$), and the total sits on the other side ($=95$).
Solve as usual: $15m+20=95 \to 15m=75 \to m=5$ months.
**Interpret**: does 5 months make sense? Positive, whole, reasonable ✓. An answer like $-3$ months would mean the setup — not the algebra — needs another look.
lessons.keyIdea: rate × unknown + one-time amount = total; then solve and sanity-check the answer.

facts.title: Interpret
facts.explanation: The answer must make sense in the story.

workedExamples.statement: A gym charges $\$20$ to join plus $\$15$/month. Total paid: $\$95$. How many months?
Three consecutive integers sum to $48$. Find them.
Two tutors: A charges $\$25$/hr; B charges $\$10$/hr plus a $\$45$ materials fee. When do they cost the same?

commonMistakes.text: Putting the one-time fee on the rate.
commonMistakes.correction: $\$20$ to join is NOT $20m$ — it doesn't repeat monthly. Rates multiply the unknown; one-time amounts stand alone.
If the problem asks for the LARGEST of three integers, $n=15$ isn't the answer — $n+2=17$ is. Reread the question at the end.

tryIt.statement: A taxi costs $\$4$ plus $\$2$ per mile. The fare was $\$18$. How many miles?
Two consecutive integers sum to $33$. Find the smaller one.

steps.title: Build it
steps.prompt: A phone plan: $\$30$ up front plus $\$10$/month, $\$80$ total. The equation is…

steps.title: Finish the story
beats: Sanity check: 5 months at \$15 is \$75, plus \$20 = \$95 ✓.

steps.title: Story to solution
steps.intro: Name the unknown, build, solve, interpret.

examples.prompt: Consecutive integers: three in a row sum to $48$. Find them.
Tutor A: $\$25$/hr. Tutor B: $\$10$/hr + $\$45$ fee. Equal cost when?

problems.prompt: Bike rental: $\$8$ plus $\$3$/hour. You paid $\$23$. Hours?
Two consecutive integers sum to $41$. The larger is…

steps.title: You solve equations now
steps.body: Two-step, cluttered, both-sides, parenthesized, special-case, and straight out of a story — all of them fall to the same moves.

steps.title: You've finished “Linear Equations”
steps.body: You can unwrap any linear equation — and diagnose the strange ones with no solution or infinitely many. Next: **Linear Functions**, where these equations become LINES and slope enters the picture.

practice.statement: A movie plan costs $\$6$ plus $\$3$ per rental; you paid $\$27$. How many rentals?

testYourself.solution: Infinitely many — both sides are identical.

testYourself.statement: Three consecutive integers sum to $72$. Find the largest.
